text_blocks.text: The 1960s would be marked by the rise of
family-oriented series in an attempt by ABC to counterprogram
its established competitors, but the decade was also marked by the
network's gradual transition to color. On September 30, 1960,
ABC premiered The Flintstones, another example of
counterprogramming; although the animated series from William
Hanna and Joseph Barbera was filmed in color from the
beginning, it was initially broadcast in black-and-white, as ABC
had not made the necessary technical upgrades to broadcast its
programming in color at the time. The Flintstones allowed ABC
to present a novelty, that of prime-time animated programming, but
it also allowed the network to begin filling the hole opened by
the conclusion of the Disney partnership by carrying
